title: Pritzker signs Illinois AI Safety Measures Act, the strongest US state AI law yet
subtitle: SB 315 forces AI developers with more than $500M in revenue to publish catastrophic-risk disclosures, submit to independent third-party audits, and face civil penalties up to $3M — effective January 1, 2028.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Illinois governor JB Pritzker signed Senate Bill 315, the Artificial Intelligence Safety Measures Act, into law on July 6. The law targets developers of the largest advanced AI systems — companies with more than $500 million in annual gross revenue — and requires them to publicly disclose safety practices, report significant safety incidents, publish explanations of how their products could pose a catastrophic risk and how those risks would be addressed, and submit to annual independent third-party audits. It creates confidential reporting channels and whistleblower protections for employees raising AI safety concerns. Non-compliance carries civil penalties of $1 million for a first violation and $3 million for each subsequent one. The requirements take effect on January 1, 2028.

The annual independent third-party audit is the piece that has not existed in US AI law before. California's SB 53 landed on transparency reporting. New York's frontier bill focused on incident disclosure. Illinois goes further by requiring external auditors to verify what the developer publishes, which shifts the enforcement model from self-attestation to something closer to how the SEC treats public company financials. The whistleblower channel adds an internal enforcement mechanism that does not depend on the state discovering violations on its own — it depends on employees having a safe path to report them.

This lands in a fragmented US regulatory picture that is getting harder to navigate, not easier. The FTC on the same week proposed a policy statement arguing that state laws requiring alterations to AI model outputs may be preempted by federal Section 5 authority — Colorado's AI Act is the named example. Federal preemption legislation attached to child-safety negotiations at the White House has been on and off for months. Illinois passing a nation-leading state law on the same day the FTC is publicly floating a preemption argument is not a coincidence in timing; it is a jurisdictional collision that will be litigated in the second half of 2026. The 2028 effective date buys the state time to see how the preemption fight resolves.

A takeaway for learners: US AI regulation right now works exactly the way US environmental and financial regulation worked historically — states experiment first, the federal government follows or preempts, and the biggest companies end up building compliance to whichever standard is strictest. If you are considering a career in AI policy or AI compliance, learn how state-level regulatory experiments turn into national floors. The Illinois auditing model, whether it survives preemption or not, is the template for what serious AI safety compliance will look like at the working level.
